questions.question: In a 45-minute LinkedIn PYMK system design interview, what is the single most common reason a candidate receives 'No Hire'?
options: Jumping into design without clarifying scale, freshness, and privacy constraints, resulting in an architecture that doesn't address the actual problem.
Spending too much time on capacity estimation and not enough on the API design.
Choosing Kafka over RabbitMQ for the event pipeline.
Failing to draw a component diagram within the first 5 minutes.
questions.explanation: Interviewers report that most 'No Hire' outcomes begin in the first 5 minutes when candidates skip clarification and design a generic system that doesn't address PYMK's specific challenges (privacy, scale, freshness).

questions.question: LinkedIn's professional graph has 950M members with an average of 500 connections each. Why is real-time 2nd-degree traversal at request time impractical?
options: Each member has up to 250K 2nd-degree candidates (500 × 500), requiring hundreds of thousands of Espresso lookups per request — far exceeding a 500ms latency budget.
Espresso does not support graph traversal operations.
The 2nd-degree candidates change too frequently to cache reliably.
LinkedIn's data center network cannot route graph queries fast enough.
questions.explanation: 250K lookups per member × 30M DAU = 7.5 trillion lookups per day at request time. Pre-computation via nightly Spark batch and storage in Espresso reduces request-time work to a single key lookup.

questions.question: A candidate answers PYMK by designing only a real-time request-time graph traversal. Which rubric dimension is most penalized?
options: Pipeline Architecture — they missed the batch vs. online distinction and designed a system that cannot meet the latency budget at scale.
Ranking/ML Design — they did not describe a ranking model.
Observability — they did not propose metrics.
Privacy/Compliance — they did not mention blocked members.
questions.explanation: The core architectural insight is separating batch candidate generation (Spark, nightly) from online re-ranking (Venice features, request time). Designing only real-time traversal fails the pipeline architecture dimension.

questions.question: Under the PYMK scoring rubric, which dimension carries the same weight as Graph Design?
options: Pipeline Architecture (both are 20 points) — because both are load-bearing technical decisions with no obvious fallback.
Privacy/Compliance (both are 20 points) — because GDPR is the primary constraint.
Observability (both are 20 points) — because metrics are the most overlooked area.
Ranking/ML Design (both are 20 points) — because the model is the hardest part to get right.
questions.explanation: Graph Design and Pipeline Architecture both carry 20 points because they are the structural core of the system. A correct ranking model or observability setup cannot compensate for a fundamentally broken graph design or pipeline.

questions.question: When a PYMK candidate receives a GDPR right-to-be-forgotten request, what must the system do beyond deleting the member's Espresso node?
options: Publish a Kafka deletion event that triggers an async cleanup job to remove the member from all other members' precomputed PYMK candidate lists in Espresso, completing within 30 days.
Invalidate the member's session tokens and remove them from the search index only.
Mark the member as inactive and stop generating recommendations for them without deleting their graph data.
Immediately rebuild the nightly Spark batch job to remove the member from all recommendations.
questions.explanation: GDPR Article 17 requires complete erasure. The member appears as a recommendation in many other members' precomputed lists. A Kafka-triggered async cleanup is the scalable approach, with a 30-day SLA.

questions.question: A member with 50,000 connections (super-node) causes the nightly PYMK batch job to time out for their partition. What are two valid mitigations?
options: Cap 2nd-degree traversal to the top-K connections by affinity score, or switch to approximate nearest-neighbor search on member embeddings for super-nodes.
Exclude super-nodes from PYMK recommendations entirely.
Run a dedicated Spark cluster exclusively for super-nodes on a separate nightly schedule.
Shard the super-node's connection list across 10 partitions so each processes 5,000 connections.
questions.explanation: Full traversal of 50K × 500 = 25M 2nd-degree candidates is too expensive per member. Capping at high-affinity connections reduces work while preserving recommendation quality. ANN on embeddings avoids graph traversal entirely.

questions.question: Why is connection acceptance rate a better primary A/B metric for PYMK than click-through rate on the recommendation?
options: Click-through rate measures curiosity; connection acceptance measures intent — someone clicking 'Connect' is a stronger signal that the recommendation was valuable.
Connection acceptance rate is easier to measure because it requires no client-side instrumentation.
Click-through rate is not recorded in LinkedIn's event pipeline.
Connection acceptance rate is required by LinkedIn's privacy policy for A/B testing.
questions.explanation: A model could maximize clicks by showing surprising or attention-grabbing profiles that members don't actually want to connect with. Acceptance rate is the outcome LinkedIn actually wants to drive.

questions.question: A new member joins LinkedIn with 0 connections. What should PYMK show them?
options: Fall back to non-graph signals: same company from their profile, same school, geographic proximity, and popular members in their industry vertical — computed without 2nd-degree graph traversal.
Show no recommendations until they make at least one connection.
Show the globally most-followed LinkedIn members as default recommendations.
Prompt the member to import their email contacts and use that graph instead.
questions.explanation: Cold-start members have no 2nd-degree graph. Profile signals (company, school, location) provide a non-graph fallback that remains relevant and personalized even without connection history.
